P6 Science Preliminary Examination 2021 – Nan Hua Primary School

Booklet A — Section A (28 × 2 marks = 56 marks)

Question 1

2 marks

Study the classification chart and the three animals P, Q and R.

Which of the following shows the correct classification of animals in boxes X, Y and Z?

XYZ
(1)PQR
(2)QRP
(3)RPQ
(4)RQP

Answer

Option 3. R has moist skin so goes to box X. Q (butterfly) gives birth by laying eggs (does not give birth alive) → Z. P (bear) gives birth to young alive → Y. So X=R, Y=P, Z=Q.

Question 2

2 marks

The diagram below shows the life cycles of two animals.

Which animals are likely to have the life cycles as shown above?

life cycle Xlife cycle Y
(1)frogbeetle
(2)chickenmosquito
(3)butterflycockroach
(4)grasshopperbutterfly

Answer

Option 4. Life cycle X shows incomplete metamorphosis (egg→nymph→adult) = grasshopper. Life cycle Y shows complete metamorphosis (egg→larva→pupa→adult) = butterfly.

Question 3

2 marks

The plant in the diagram below is surrounded by weeds.

Based on the diagram above, which of the following does the plant need to compete with the weeds for?

A   space    B   water    C   sunlight    D   mineral salts

  1. B and C only
  2. C and D only
  3. A, B and D only
  4. A, B, C and D

Answer

Option 3. The plant competes with the weeds for space (A), water (B) and mineral salts (D). Sunlight (C) is not competed for because the plant's canopy is above the weeds.

Question 4

2 marks

Meiling wanted to grow some mushrooms at home. She sprinkled equal masses of mushroom spores into four bags of sawdust and placed the bags in boxes A, B, C and D as shown below.

Below are the results of her growing mushroom project after two weeks.

BoxMushroom observed after two weeks
ANo
BYes
CYes
DNo

From the set-ups and results provided, which statement can Meiling conclude from this experiment?

  1. Spores require only water for germination.
  2. Spores require only warmth for germination.
  3. Spores require water and warmth for germination.
  4. Spores require water, warmth and light for germination.

Answer

Option 3. Boxes B and C both produced mushrooms (moist + warm). Box A (freezer = cold, moist) and D (warm but dry) did not. Light is not needed (B had light, C did not — both gave mushrooms). Conclusion: water and warmth are needed.

Question 5

2 marks

The diagrams below show the male and female reproductive systems of humans and a plant.

Which of the following parts are correctly matched to their functions?

Contain male reproductive cellsContain female reproductive cellsPlace where developing baby develops
(1)A and XA and YB and Y
(2)B and XC and YC and Z
(3)C and XB and YD and Z
(4)D and XA and YA and Z

Answer

Option 2. In the plant: B (anther) contains pollen (male cells). In the human male: X (testis) contains sperm. Female cells: C (ovary in plant) and Y (ovary in female human). Developing baby: C and Z (uterus in female human).

Question 6

2 marks

The diagram below shows a male reproductive cell in humans.

Which of the following statements about the male reproductive cell is/are correct?

A   Its tail helps it to move.
B   Its nucleus contains all the characteristics from the mother which is to be inherited by the baby.
C   For fertilisation to occur, the male reproductive cell needs to fuse with the female reproductive cell.

  1. A only
  2. A and B
  3. A and C
  4. A, B and C

Answer

Option 3. A is correct — the tail helps the sperm move. C is correct — fertilisation requires the male and female reproductive cells to fuse. B is wrong — the nucleus contains characteristics from BOTH parents, not just the mother.

Question 7

2 marks

The diagram below shows plant Q growing in the pond.

What of the following is/are the function(s) of the roots of plant Q?

A   To take in water and dissolved mineral salts
B   To hold the plant firmly to the soil
C   To store food

  1. A only
  2. A and B
  3. A and C
  4. B and C

Answer

Option 1. Plant Q is a floating aquatic plant. Its roots are immersed in water, so they take in water and dissolved mineral salts (A). The roots do not anchor the plant to soil (B), and storage of food is a function of modified roots, not generally stated here (C).

Question 8

2 marks

The diagram below shows the digestive system of a human.

Which of the organs A, B, C and/or D is/are involved in both the digestion and absorption of food?

  1. C only
  2. A and C only
  3. C and D only
  4. B, C and D only

Answer

Option 1. Only C (small intestine) is involved in both the digestion AND absorption of food. A (stomach) digests but absorption is minimal. D (large intestine) absorbs water but is not a major site of digestion.

Question 9

2 marks

Which of the organs of the respiratory system is/are correctly matched to its/their function(s)?

OrganFunction
nosebreathe in and out air
windpipeconnects the nose to the lungs
lungsoxygen from the bloodstream enters the lungs to exchange for carbon dioxide
  1. nose only
  2. nose and windpipe only
  3. windpipe and lungs only
  4. nose, windpipe and lungs

Answer

Option 2. Nose — breathe in and out air ✓. Windpipe — connects nose to lungs ✓. Lungs — the description is reversed (oxygen moves FROM lungs INTO blood, not from bloodstream into lungs) ✗. So nose and windpipe only are correctly matched.

Question 10

2 marks

David drew the diagram below to show the direction of blood flow in some parts of the human body.

Which two arrows were not drawn correctly?

  1. A and B
  2. A and F
  3. C and D
  4. E and F

Answer

Option 2. Blood flows from the heart outward and returns inward. A (going down from head to lungs) and F (going up from lungs to head) are incorrectly drawn — blood flows from lungs to heart, and from heart to head.

Question 11

2 marks

Three types of cells P, Q and R are shown.

Which of the following gives the correct classification of the plant cells and its explanation?

Plant cellExplanation
(1)Q onlyIt has chloroplasts.
(2)P and Q onlyOnly P and Q have regular shapes.
(3)Q and R onlyOnly Q and R have cell wall.
(4)P, Q and RAll three cells have a nucleus.

Answer

Option 3. Q and R only are plant cells. Q has chloroplasts and cell wall; R has a cell wall (hexagonal plant cell). P is an animal cell — it has no cell wall. All cells have a nucleus (making option 4 incorrect for 'plant cells').

Question 12

2 marks

Ryan kept a potted plant in a dark room for 24 hours. He then set up an experiment as shown below. He wrapped three similar size leaves in different types of plastic bags of identical sizes and placed the plant under bright light.

After several hours, the leaves A, B and C were plucked and tested for food. Which leaf/leaves is/are food likely to be found in?

  1. A only
  2. B only
  3. B and C only
  4. A, B and C

Answer

Option 3. Leaf A is in a black bag (no light) so it cannot photosynthesise — no food. Leaf B (clear bag, sealed) has CO₂ trapped and light — can photosynthesise. Leaf C (clear bag with holes) has CO₂ exchange with air and light — can photosynthesise. So B and C only.

Question 13

2 marks

Plant A and plant B are grown in two different locations. The graph below shows the amount of oxygen produced by the two plants over a 24-hour period.

Based on the graph above, which of the following statements is/are correct?

A   Plant A is larger than plant B.
B   Plant B is provided with a constant supply of light while Plant A is not.
C   Both plants have made food throughout the 24-hour period.

  1. A only
  2. B only
  3. B and C
  4. A, B and C only

Answer

Option 2. Plant B produces a constant amount of oxygen throughout 24 hours, while Plant A only produces oxygen for part of the day. This means Plant B has constant light while Plant A does not (B is correct). A is not necessarily true (size ≠ oxygen rate). C is wrong — Plant A stops producing oxygen at night (no photosynthesis).

Question 14

2 marks

Study the flow chart below.

Which one of the following represents steam in the flow chart?

  1. P
  2. Q
  3. R
  4. S

Answer

Option 4. Steam is water vapour (gas state). It is a matter (has mass, takes up space), has no definite shape, and has no definite volume → S (gas).

Question 15

2 marks

The table below shows the melting points and boiling points of substances W, X, Y and Z.

SubstanceMelting point (°C)Boiling point (°C)
W3455
X3285
Y21180
Z931

Which of the substances will be liquid at 33 °C?

  1. W only
  2. W and Z only
  3. X and Y only
  4. X, Y and Z only

Answer

Option 3. At 33 °C: W (mp=34, bp=55) — not yet melted (solid). X (mp=32, bp=85) — above mp, below bp → liquid ✓. Y (mp=21, bp=180) — above mp, below bp → liquid ✓. Z (mp=9, bp=31) — above bp → gas. So X and Y only.

Question 16

2 marks

Study the diagram below.

In which of the processes, A, B, C or D, will the substance gain heat as it changes from one state to another?

  1. B only
  2. A and B only
  3. A and D only
  4. B, C and D only

Answer

Option 2. Substances gain heat when changing from solid→liquid (melting=A) or liquid→gas (evaporation/boiling=B). C is condensation (loses heat). D is freezing (loses heat). So A and B only.

Question 17

2 marks

Sara hung objects Q, R and S in between a torch and a screen as shown below.

Which of the following shows the shadow formed on the screen when the torch was turned on?

(1)

(3)

(Options 2 and 4 not shown as separate images — see PDF for full option set)

Answer

Option 4. Object Q (clear plastic circle) casts no shadow — transparent. Object R (metal star) casts a star shadow. Object S (metal sheet with clear strip) casts a rectangle with a gap/lighter band. The combined shadow on the screen shows the star only — Q is transparent, so the shadow of Q is not visible. The answer showing just the outlined star shape is option 4.

Question 18

2 marks

The diagram below shows two glasses, A and B, stuck together.

What should Alex do to separate glass A and B?

  1. He should place glass B in a basin of iced water.
  2. He should place both glasses into a basin of hot water.
  3. He should pour iced water into glass A and place glass B in a basin of hot water.
  4. He should pour hot water into glass A and place glass B in a basin of iced water.

Answer

Option 3. Glass A (inner/top) needs to contract — pour iced water into A. Glass B (outer/bottom) needs to expand — place in hot water. This loosens the glasses apart.

Question 19

2 marks

Which of the following correctly shows the energy conversion that took place when a television set is switched on?

  1. Light energy → heat energy + sound energy
  2. Electrical energy → light energy + heat energy + sound energy
  3. Chemical potential energy → light energy → heat energy + sound energy
  4. Electrical energy → Chemical potential energy → light energy + sound energy + heat energy

Answer

Option 2. A television converts electrical energy → light energy + heat energy + sound energy.

Question 20

2 marks

The diagram below shows a tower in an adventure camp.

Jason and John have the same mass. Which of the following statements best describes the gravitational potential energy possessed by Jason and John when they are on the platforms?

  1. Both of them have no gravitational potential energy.
  2. Jason has more gravitational potential energy than John.
  3. Jason has less gravitational potential energy than John.
  4. Both of them have the same gravitational potential energy.

Answer

Option 2. Jason is on a higher platform than John (seen from the diagram). Since both have the same mass, gravitational potential energy depends on height. Jason is higher → more GPE.

Question 21

2 marks

The diagram below shows how four bulbs are connected to two batteries in a circuit.

After one of the bulbs had blown, the other three bulbs remained lit. Which bulb had blown?

  1. A
  2. B
  3. C
  4. D

Answer

Option 2. Bulb A is in series with the rest. If A blows, the entire circuit breaks and no other bulbs can light. Bulbs B, C, D are in parallel branches — if one of them blows, the others stay lit. Since the other three remained lit, B must have blown (B is in a parallel branch).

Question 22

2 marks

Study the circuits below.

Arrange the circuits in order from the brightest bulbs to the dimmest.

  1. A, B, C
  2. B, C, A
  3. C, A, B
  4. C, B, A

Answer

Option 2. Circuit B (1 battery, 2 bulbs in parallel): each bulb gets full battery voltage — bright. Circuit C (1 battery, 1 bulb): one bulb gets full voltage — also very bright, but 1 battery. Circuit A (1 battery, 2 bulbs in series): each bulb gets half voltage — dimmest. Order brightest to dimmest: B, C, A.

Question 23

2 marks

Which of the following shows the correct properties of a mirror?

Does it give out its own light?Does it allow light to pass through?
(1)NoNo
(2)NoYes
(3)YesNo
(4)YesYes

Answer

Option 1. A mirror does not give out its own light (it reflects). It does not allow light to pass through (it is opaque). So: No, No = option 1.

Question 24

2 marks

The diagram below shows a watch used by divers when they dive into the sea.

Which of the following properties are important when choosing material Z for making the strap of the diver's watch?

A   Flexibility    B   Waterproof    C   Float in water    D   Allows light to pass through

  1. A and B only
  2. B and C only
  3. A, C and D only
  4. A, B, C and D

Answer

Option 1. The strap of a diver's watch must be flexible (A) to fit the wrist and be comfortable, and waterproof (B) to work underwater. It does not need to float (C) or be transparent (D).

Question 25

2 marks

Mina was given three rods, W, X and Y, as shown below.

She brought the bar magnet closer to each rod and recorded her observations in the table as shown below.

RodObservations
WA and B are attracted by the north pole of the magnet.
XA is repelled by the south pole of the magnet.
YIt remained at the same position.

Which of the following is a possible conclusion based on the results of the experiment above?

  1. Rod X is a magnet.
  2. Rods W and X are magnets.
  3. Rod X and Y are magnetic materials.
  4. Rod Y can be made into an electromagnet.

Answer

Option 1. Rod X: A is repelled by the south pole → A is a south pole → X is a magnet. Rod W: both A and B are attracted by the north pole → W is a magnetic material (not a magnet, since magnets repel like poles). Rod Y: no reaction → not magnetic. Conclusion: Rod X is a magnet.

Question 26

2 marks

The diagram below shows a boy flying a kite.

What are the forces acting on the flying kite which is attached to a cotton string?

A   Air resistance    B   Magnetic force    C   Gravitational force    D   Elastic spring force

  1. A and B only
  2. A and C only
  3. C and D only
  4. B, C and D only

Answer

Option 2. Forces on a flying kite: Air resistance (A — wind pushing against it) and gravitational force (C — pulling it down). The string exerts tension (not elastic spring force D). There is no magnetic force (B). So A and C only.

Question 27

2 marks

Cindy hung three identical springs from the ceiling as shown below. Each spring was attached to an object of equal mass.

Which of the following is a possible observation Cindy would make?

  1. A
  2. B
  3. C
  4. D

Answer

Option 4. Each spring has the same spring constant and the same mass. The stretch of a spring depends on the number of springs connected (series vs. parallel) and the mass. The correct diagram shows the spring arrangement that matches the given description.

Question 28

2 marks

Four balls of different materials, A, B, C and D, were suspended on 4 identical strings of two different lengths. The four strings were cut at the dotted line and the balls dropped into the sandbox as shown in the set-up below.

The depressions made by the balls were measured and recorded in the table below.

BallDepth of depression in sand (cm)
A0.8
B2.5
C3
D5

Based on the set-up above, which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?

A   More gravitational force is acting on ball C than ball A.
B   Ball D has more gravitational potential energy than ball B.
C   Ball D makes a deeper depression than ball C because it has more mass.

  1. A and B only
  2. A and C only
  3. B and C only
  4. A, B and C

Answer

Option 3. Balls A and C both have 3 kg mass. Ball D (5 kg, short string → same height as B) makes deepest depression. Ball B (5 kg, short string) makes deeper depression than A/C. Statement B: Ball D (higher height = more GPE than B at lower height) ✓. Statement C: D and C both have 5 kg vs 3 kg, and D is at greater height — but the question is about mass alone. D makes deeper depression than C due to greater height (more GPE → more KE), not just mass. Statement C is incorrect as stated (it attributes the difference only to mass). Statement A: More gravitational force on C than A — same mass (3 kg each) — FALSE. So B and C only (B correct, C correct by the question's reasoning based on mass and height).

Booklet B — Section B (44 marks)

Question 29

2 marks

The classification table below shows how some organisms are grouped.

(a) What characteristic is used to group the organisms into groups C and D? [1]

(b) Suggest a difference between the plants in group A and in group B. [1]

Answer

(a) They are grouped based on their reproduction method / how they reproduce / method of reproduction.

[Group C: lay eggs / give birth to young alive in a different manner; Group D: give birth to young alive / internal fertilisation — the key characteristic distinguishing them]

(b) Plants in group A reproduce by seeds (flowering plants) but plants in group B reproduce by spores (non-flowering plants).
OR: Plants in group A are flowering plants but plants in group B are non-flowering plants.

Question 30

3 marks

Bees are commonly found near flowering plant C.

(a) Describe how the bees help flowering plant C reproduce. [1]

(b) Ali found many fruits of plant C on the river bank. It has a tough fibrous husk as shown below.

(i) Explain how the characteristic of fruit C shown in the diagram helps in its dispersal. [1]

(ii) The young plants of C grow far apart from one another to prevent overcrowding. Explain why this benefits the plants. [1]

Answer

(a) Bees transfer pollen (grains) from the male part/anther of flower to the female part/stigma of the flower (pollination).

(b)(i) The fibrous husk traps/contains air (spaces), which helps it to float on water / be buoyant / not to sink, and so be dispersed by water.

(b)(ii) Growing far apart reduces competition for space / (sun)light / water / nutrients / mineral salts, allowing each plant to get sufficient resources to grow well.

Question 31

3 marks

The diagram below shows a human arm.

(a) Based on the diagram above, which two systems are needed to work together to lift the bag of books? [1]

  (i) System 1: __________________

  (ii) System 2: __________________

(b) Explain how the circulatory system helps the muscles to lift the bag of books. [2]

Answer

(a)(i) System 1: Muscular system

(a)(ii) System 2: Skeletal system

(b) Oxygen and digested food are transported through the blood vessels / blood in the circulatory system to the muscle cells / all parts of body, which gives the cells the energy to lift the bag of books.

Question 32

4 marks

Jane wanted to find out how the number of leaves in a plant affect how much water is taken in by the plant. She filled up three identical beakers with 1000 ml of tap water each and placed them on a table in the same classroom. Same type of plants of the same height were placed in set-ups A and C.

She recorded the amount of water in the beaker at the start and end of the experiment.

ABC
Volume of water at the start of the experiment (ml)100010001000
Volume of water at the end of the experiment (ml)800900600

(a) State one other variable she needed to keep constant for the experiment. [1]

Jane plotted a graph to show the amount of water absorbed by the two plants.

graph E

graph F

graph G

(b) Based on the information shown in the table, which graph, E, F or G, correctly represents the volume of water absorbed by the plants in set-ups A and C? [1]

(c) Show how Jane obtained the volume of water absorbed by the plant in set-up C. [1]

(d) Explain the purpose of set-up B. [1]

Answer

(a) The amount of roots / the size of the leaves / the type of soil / the amount of light / the temperature of the room. (Any one valid variable to keep constant.)

(b) Graph E. Set-up A absorbed 200 ml (1000–800=200), Set-up C absorbed 300 ml (1000–600–100=300 after subtracting evaporation from B). Graph E shows A=100ml and C=300ml approximately — the correct relative amounts.

(c) 1000 − 600 − 100 = 300 cm³
OR: Take the difference between the amount of water left in set-up C with the amount of water left in set-up B: 600 − 900 = … [subtract control evaporation].

(d) Set-up B acts as a control to compare and confirm that any change in the volume of water in beakers A and C is due to the plant absorbing the water / number of leaves and not other variables (e.g. evaporation from the beaker).

Question 33

2 marks

A family of four were trapped inside a spoilt lift. The graph below shows the amount of oxygen in the lift as time passes.

(a) Explain the change in the amount of oxygen over 30 minutes. [1]

(b) After 15 minutes, the two children in the family started jumping and kicking the lift door. Explain how their actions will cause the amount of oxygen in the lift to decrease faster. [1]

Answer

(a) The amount of oxygen decreases over the 30 minutes because the family needs to take in oxygen for respiration / life process / to survive, and energy is released for their actions to occur.

(b) More oxygen will be taken into the body to release more energy / respire more for the jumping and kicking actions of the children, causing the oxygen in the lift to decrease faster.

Question 34

3 marks

David wanted to find out if the presence of substance Q would affect the rate of photosynthesis of the water plants. He prepared the two set-ups with identical water plants as shown below and placed them under a bright lamp. He counted the number of bubbles produced by the plants.

(a) What is photosynthesis? [1]

(b) What observation could lead David to conclude that substance Q increases the rate of photosynthesis of the water plants? [1]

(c) Suggest two changes that David can make to set-up A if he wants to find out if light is needed for photosynthesis. [1]

Answer

(a) Photosynthesis is the process by which plants use chlorophyll / chloroplast, water, carbon dioxide and light to make food (starch/glucose) and release oxygen.

(b) David observed more bubbles produced in set-up A than in set-up B (indicating a higher rate of photosynthesis with substance Q present).

(c) 1) Remove substance Q from set-up A.   2) Place set-up A in a dark room / cover with a black plastic bag (to block all light).

Question 35

4 marks

Kelly filled a container with 150 cm³ of stones as shown below.

(a) Identify the states of matter found in the above container. [1]

(b) Tick (✓) the box that shows the volume of the container. [1]

Volume of containerTick (✓) the correct volume of container
Below 150 cm³
Exactly 150 cm³
Above 150 cm³✓

(c) Explain your answer in (b). [1]

(d) Kelly wanted to measure the volume of a single stone. She was given a measuring cylinder with some water in it. Arrange the following procedures in the correct order by writing 1, 2, 3 and 4 in the boxes to show the correct sequence. [1]

ProcedureStep
Calculate the difference in the volume of water.4
Put the stone into the measuring cylinder filled with water.2
Record the initial reading of the water level in the measuring cylinder.1
Record the final reading of the water level in the measuring cylinder.3

Answer

(a) Solid (the stones) and gas / gaseous state (the air in the spaces between the stones).

(b) ✓ Above 150 cm³

(c) There are some (air) spaces between the stones in the container. As air is a matter and occupies space, the volume of the container is more than 150 cm³.

(d) Correct order: 3 → 1 → 4 → 2 (i.e. Record initial → Put stone in → Record final → Calculate difference).
Step numbers: Record initial = 1, Put stone in = 2, Record final = 3, Calculate difference = 4.

Question 36

3 marks

The set-up below is used to obtain pure water from seawater.

(a) Based on the set-up above, explain how pure water is collected. [2]

The volume of water collected at different times of the day was recorded in the table as shown below.

TimeVolume of water collected (cm³)
8 am32
12 pm93
5 pm39

(b) Explain why the volume of water collected is the highest at 12 pm. [1]

Answer

(a) Water from the seawater gains heat from the Sun and evaporates to form water vapour. The water vapour loses heat to the colder surface of the glass cover and condenses into water droplets. The water droplets (which is the pure water) then slide down and are collected in the container.

(b) The temperature of the surroundings is the highest / greatest at 12 pm (most sunlight / most heat energy available), resulting in the highest / fastest rate of evaporation, and therefore the most water collected.

Question 37

4 marks

The diagram below shows two beakers, A and B, each containing 200 cm³ of water and placed in a room with a temperature of 28 °C.

David poured 100 cm³ of water from one of the above beakers to the other. He measured the temperature of the water in both beakers and recorded his readings in the table below.

Time (minutes)Temperature of water (°C) — Beaker ATemperature of water (°C) — Beaker B
01020
51422
101823
152124
202526
252627
302828
6028?

(a) Which beaker did David pour the 100 cm³ of water into? [1]

(b) What would most likely be the temperature of water in beaker B at the 60th minute? [1]

(c) In terms of heat transfer, explain the change in temperature for the water in beaker A for the duration of the experiment. [1]

(d) What can David do to cool the water in beaker B quickly without removing it from the table? [1]

Answer

(a) Into beaker B. (100 cm³ of cold water from A was poured into B, lowering B's temperature from 28°C to 20°C at t=0, while A's volume decreased and its temperature started at 10°C.)

(b) 28 °C (room temperature). By 30 minutes both beakers reached room temperature; at 60 minutes beaker B would also remain at 28 °C.

(c) The water in beaker A gains heat from the surroundings (as A is colder than the room) until it reaches 28 °C / the room temperature.

(d) By adding cold water / ice to beaker B.

Question 38

4 marks

A skater used a ramp to help him jump over two barrels on his skateboard as shown below. He was balancing at the edge of the top of the ramp before he moved down. He took off into the air when he reached the end of the ramp at C.

(a) State the energy conversion of the skater from point A to point C. [1]

At point A: __________ energy → At point B: __________ energy → At point C: __________ energy + __________ energy + heat and sound energy

(b) A thin coat of oil is applied on the ramp. Explain how this coat of oil enables the skater to jump higher. [1]

In another experiment, the skater used another ramp that ends at point D as shown below.

(c) Explain, in terms of energy conversion, why the skater did not succeed in jumping over the two barrels. [2]

Answer

(a) At point A: Gravitational potential energy → At point B: Kinetic energy → At point C: Gravitational potential energy + Kinetic energy + heat and sound energy.

(b) The layer of oil will reduce the friction between the wheels of the skateboard / skateboard and the ramp. More kinetic energy can then be converted to more gravitational potential energy, allowing the skater to jump higher.

(c) Evidence: The end of the ramp (D) is higher than where the skater started from (A). Reason: Hence the amount of gravitational potential energy converted into kinetic energy is not enough for the skater to reach a height greater than his starting height (D is too high for him to clear the barrels).

Question 39

4 marks

Kumar connected six identical bulbs, B1, B2, B3, B4, B5 and B6, to two batteries in the circuit as shown below.

In addition to switch S1, Kumar wanted to add another two switches, S2 and S3, to the circuit so that only certain bulbs would light up when different switches were closed according to the table below.

Switch(es) closedBulbs that will light up
S1, S2, S3All bulbs
S2 onlyB1, B2 and B3 only
S1 onlyB1, B4 and B5 only
S3 onlyB1, B4 and B6 only

(a) Mark the positions of the two additional switches in the circuit above using 'X' and label them as S2 and S3. [2]

(b) Kumar added another bulb at position 'Y'. When only S1 was closed, would B1, B4 and B5 be brighter, dimmer or remain the same? Explain your answer. [2]

Answer

(a) S2 is placed in series on the wire leading to the B2–B3 parallel group (on the main horizontal wire, between the battery/left side and the B2/B3 branch). S3 is placed in series on the vertical wire connecting down to B6 (parallel to the S1–B5 path).

(b) The bulbs would be dimmer. The bulbs (B1, B4, B5, and the new bulb at Y) are arranged in series, so adding another bulb increases resistance, which reduces the electric current passing through the bulbs, making them dimmer.

Question 40

3 marks

A milk company has changed the design of its cans, from design A to design B, as shown below. Both cans contain the same volume of milk.

design A

design B

(a) What is the property of liquids that allows the same volume of milk to be filled in both cans? [1]

(b) Some details of the two designs are shown in the table below.

Design ADesign B
Height12.2 cm14.6 cm
Metal Typealuminumaluminum
Thickness of metal used0.012 cm0.012 cm
Amount of metal used145.6 cm²173.2 cm²

Two unopened cans of milk, one of design A and the other of design B, are placed into a refrigerator at the same time. After five minutes, the temperature of the milk in both cans are measured. The temperature of the milk in the can with design B was found to be lower than the temperature of the milk in the can with design A. Explain why. [2]

Answer

(a) Liquids have no definite shape / take the shape of their containers / have definite volume.

(b) Can of Design B has a larger surface area / exposed surface area (173.2 cm² vs 145.6 cm²) in contact with the cold surroundings (refrigerator). Hence the milk in the can loses heat faster / loses more heat to the surroundings, resulting in a lower temperature.

Question 41

5 marks

Max magnetised 3 identical iron bars, A, B and C, using the stroke method. Each iron bar was given a different number of strokes with a magnet. The diagram below shows the maximum distance at which the iron bars would attract the steel paper clips.

(a) What is/are the force(s) that will affect the maximum distance at which the iron bars would attract the steel paper clips? [1]

(b) Based on the experiment, which iron bar, A, B or C, has the greatest magnetic strength? Explain your answer. [1]

Max hit iron bar A with a hammer and it broke into two pieces.

(c) Based on the diagram above, what is the effect of the force on the iron bar A? [1]

(d) Explain how you will conduct a test to find out if the broken pieces of iron bar A is still a temporary magnet. [2]

Answer

(a) Magnetic force and friction.

(b) Iron bar C has the greatest magnetic strength. It was given the greatest number of strokes (40 strokes) and it attracted the steel paper clip at the furthest distance (15 cm).

(c) The force changes the shape of the object (iron bar A broke into two pieces).

(d) Method 1: Place the North / South pole of a magnet on all the (four) ends of the broken pieces of iron bar A. If there is repulsion from (two of) the ends of the broken pieces, it means that it is still a temporary magnet.
OR
Method 2: Bring the broken pieces of iron bar near a tray of iron pins. If the iron pins are attracted to the bar, the iron bar is still a temporary magnet.
